C[Fairgo: When Hiakru no Go first made its way to the USA there was an uptick in the number of youth players who started playing. While many stopped playing after the hype of the anime died down, a lot still continued playing which was a boon for the American Go Association (AGA)

Fairgo: Also, as a result of so many new young dan players, the AGA was able to devise a US pro system with the Nihon Ki-in. Albeit, AGA pros are still substantially weaker than pros in Japan, China, Korea etc.
Fairgo: The AlphaGo vs. Lee Sedol 5 game series in March (along with the subsequent media coverage) also drew a lot of new casual players into the fold.
Fairgo: As a result of this the US Go Congress is going to have a record attendance this year!  :) You can see more info about it here: http://www.gocongress.org/

Fairgo: Overall, the USA does have much fewer players than Japan, however recently more people have at least heard about the game. Also, the average player age here is late 30s - early 40s I believe, which is lower than it was in the past (primarily due to the Hikaru no Go effect)

Fairgo: I'm a bit surprised that the main player age in Japan is that high, but I guess it's to be expected now that there are so many different forms of entertainment out there that didn't exist to the same degree in the past (e.g. video games).
